Times of trouble must be times of prayer; when God seems distant from us, we must seek him until we find him. When the psalmist was in trouble, he did not look for distractions or entertainment, but he sought God and his favor and grace. Those who are troubled in mind must pray to have it removed. He focused on the trouble; the methods that should have eased him only increased his grief. When he remembered God, it was only God’s justice and wrath. His spirit was overwhelmed and sank under the burden. But let not the memory of the comforts we have lost make us ungrateful for those that remain. He especially recalled the comforts with which he sustained himself in past sorrows. Here is the language of a sorrowful, abandoned soul, walking in darkness; a common experience even among those who fear the Lord. Nothing wounds and pierces like the thought of God’s anger. God's own people, in a dark and troubled time, may be tempted to make incorrect conclusions about their spiritual state and that of God’s kingdom in the world. But we must not give way to such fears. Let faith answer them from Scripture. A troubled spring will clear itself again; and remembering times of joyful experience often raises hope for relief. Doubts and fears come from a lack and weakness of faith. Despair and distrust under affliction are too often the weaknesses of believers, and as such, we should think about them with sorrow and shame. When unbelief is working in us, we must suppress its rise.

Remembering God’s works will be a powerful remedy against distrust of his promise and goodness; for he is God, and does not change. God's way is in the sanctuary. We are sure that God is holy in all his works. God’s ways are like deep waters, which cannot be understood; like the path of a ship, which cannot be tracked. God brought Israel out of Egypt. This was typical of the great redemption to be accomplished in due time, both by price and power. If we have entertained doubtful thoughts, we should without delay turn our minds to meditate on that God who did not spare his own Son, but delivered him up for us all, so that with him he might freely give us all things.
